name: Lily Pond
short_description: Scenic pond area in the Hampshire Hills with opportunities to find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Gentle sloping terrain makes this an accessible spot for casual rockhounders exploring glaci...
description:
Lily Pond sits within the Hampshire Hills region of south-central Massachusetts, an area shaped by the Ordovician and Devonian rock formations that underlie much of western Massachusetts. This location offers rockhounders access to the metamorphic and pegmatitic minerals typical of New England's Acadian orogeny landscape. The gentle slope and accessible terrain around the pond make it ideal for families and beginners interested in identifying common New England mineral specimens.

The area is characterized by glacially-derived surficial deposits overlying Paleozoic metamorphic bedrock. Quartz is abundant here in various forms, from milky white crystals to colored varieties. Feldspar, particularly plagioclase and potassium feldspar, is common in pegmatitic outcrops and weathered granitic debris. Mica sheets can be found in schists and pegmatites, and tourmaline occasionally appears in these same pegmatitic zones.

Visitors should expect mixed woodland and open areas around the pond. The elevation of 1,451 feet provides moderate relief without being strenuous. Spring runoff and seasonal water levels affect access around the pond margins. The gentle slope makes ground exploration relatively easy, though you will encounter typical New England rocky and rooty terrain.

This location works well for day trips from nearby towns and offers a rewarding combination of scenic beauty and mineralogical interest. Collectors should focus on areas where glacial erratics have concentrated, along the pond margins, and in any visible bedrock outcrops on the hillsides.
history: Lily Pond lies within the Berkshire Mountains and Hampshire Hills, a region shaped by the Acadian orogeny during the Paleozoic Era when continental collision metamorphosed sedimentary and igneous rocks. The glacial epochs of the Pleistocene then scoured this landscape, exposing metamorphic bedrock and scattering mineral-rich glacial erratics across the terrain, creating the accessible collecting conditions found here today.
tips: Search the pond margins and surrounding hillsides where glacial action has concentrated mineral specimens, particularly in areas with exposed bedrock outcrops and along seasonal water wash lines. Clear quartz crystals and feldspar fragments are easiest to spot on bare ground; look carefully in leaf litter and around tree roots where minerals may have weathered out. Bring a hand lens to examine mica sheets and tourmaline needles within pegmatite fragments, and use a small chisel to carefully extract specimens from accessible outcrops rather than hammering indiscriminately.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,451 feet with gentle slope throughout the area. Mixed deciduous woodland and open spaces surround the pond. Terrain is typical New England granite-based bedrock with glacial deposits, rocky footing, and exposed roots. Walking distance from parking to prime collecting areas varies from 100 feet to half mile depending on access point. Sturdy hiking boots recommended; trails may be muddy in wet seasons.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall offers the best combination of dry ground, accessible trails, and low water levels at the pond margins. Summer provides longest daylight but can be buggy. Fall foliage season (September - October) offers pleasant conditions and natural exposure of minerals through leaf fall and erosion.
written_directions: From Goshen center, take Massachusetts Route 9 northwest approximately 2 miles. Turn right onto a local road toward the pond (local road names vary; use GPS coordinates 42.447325, -72.831876 as primary navigation). Follow to the small parking area near the pond. The location is accessible by passenger vehicle on well-maintained roads; final approach may be unpaved depending on parking area development.
road_conditions: Paved roads to general area; final approach may be gravel or unpaved. Adequate clearance for passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Small informal parking area near pond. Limited capacity, typically 3-5 vehicles. No designated parking lot; use area responsibly and do not block access roads.
collection_rules: Rockhounding is permitted for personal use only on public land and pond surroundings. Collect only loose specimens and surface materials. Do not excavate into bedrock or disturb natural features. Respect private property boundaries. Remove only what you can carry. Leave no trash. Check with local town authorities if boundary questions arise.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial activity
No excavation or hammering into bedrock
Remove only loose surface specimens
Respect private property boundaries
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Goshen center, approximately 2 miles. Small village with basic services. Larger services (grocery, dining, fuel) in Worthington or Cummington, 5-8 miles away.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slope accessible to most hikers. Parking to collecting areas requires walking on natural ground with rocks and roots typical of New England forest. No wheelchair access to main collecting areas. Sturdy footwear strongly recommended. Terrain difficulty is low, but footing can be uneven and wet in spring.

faq.question: What minerals can you find rockhounding at Lily Pond in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Lily Pond is known for quartz (including clear and milky varieties), feldspar, mica sheets, and occasional tourmaline. These minerals are typical of the metamorphic bedrock and pegmatites underlying the Hampshire Hills region, plus glacial erratics that have been scattered across the area.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Lily Pond free and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Lily Pond is free and does not require a permit. Personal use collection of loose surface specimens is permitted on public land surrounding the pond. Commercial collection and excavation are not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Goshen, Massachusetts?
faq.answer: May through October is ideal, with best conditions from late September through October when trails are dry, water levels are lower, and natural mineral exposure is highest. Spring can be muddy, and winter weather makes access difficult.

faq.question: Is Lily Pond rockhounding site good for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Lily Pond is excellent for beginners and families. The gentle slope, easy terrain, and abundance of quartz and feldspar make it ideal for teaching mineral identification. Children ages 6 and up can comfortably participate. Dogs are also welcome on the trails.

faq.question: How far is Lily Pond from the nearest town and what tools do I need?
faq.answer: Lily Pond is approximately 2 miles from Goshen center, making it a convenient day trip. Bring a hand lens for examining specimens, a small chisel for careful extraction from outcrops, a bucket or collecting bag, and sturdy boots. A geological hammer is optional but not necessary for surface collecting.

faq.question: Can you drive a regular car to Lily Pond rockhounding area?
faq.answer: Yes, a passenger vehicle is suitable for accessing Lily Pond. The main roads to the area are paved, and parking is available near the pond. The final approach may be unpaved but is passable for standard vehicles. No all-terrain vehicle is required.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Lily Pond near Goshen, MA. Free public collecting on accessible terrain. Perfect for families and beginners.
